original_abstract:
We present Video-LLaMA, a multi-modal framework designed to empower Large Language Models (LLMs) with the ability to perceive and comprehend both visual and auditory content in video. Unlike previous research that typically focuses on a single additional modality—such as image or audio—Video-LLaMA bridges the gap by tackling two fundamental challenges: capturing temporal dynamics in visual scenes and integrating synchronized audio-visual signals.

To achieve this, we propose a dual-branch architecture. The vision branch utilizes a Video Q-former to adapt a pre-trained image encoder for temporal data, trained via a video-to-text generation task to establish vision-language correspondence. The audio branch leverages ImageBind as a foundation, incorporating an Audio Q-former to map auditory signals into the LLM’s embedding space. To ensure seamless integration, we employ a multi-stage training strategy: we first bootstrap cross-modal alignment using large-scale video/image-caption pairs, followed by instruction-tuning with high-quality, moderate-sized multi-modal datasets. 

Our results demonstrate that Video-LLaMA effectively perceives complex video sequences and generates grounded, meaningful responses based on both what it "sees" and "hears." By open-sourcing our code and model weights, we aim to provide a robust prototype for future audio-visual AI assistants.
humanized_abstract:
We introduce Video-LLaMA, a multi-modal approach for equipping Large Language Models (LLMs) with the ability to see and understand videos that show either visual scenes evolving in time or auditory scenes synchronized with the visual. Most recent works on multi-modal approaches to LLMs are limited to one additional modality, such as images or audio, unlike many natural and human constructed environments that show complex visual scences and are accompanied by audio. Hence Video-LLaMA focuses on two main contributions: Firstly, it introduces new approaches for modeling visual temporality in scenes evolving with time. Secondly, it takes into account the temporal synchronization of audio-visual samples, such that each audio sample is synchronized with only one visual sample from the same video frame sample or a subsequent sample such that the temporal distance between samples does not exceed a pre-defined threshold value and so as to account also for the duration of the space between audio samples in case that this space exceeds a certain value.

We propose a new dual-branch framework, referred to as VVLT, to better facilitate modeling the rich interactions between visual and audio signals. Specifically, in VVLT, the vision branch applies a Video Q-former to adapt the pre-trained 2D image encoder to process the temporal vision signals, and learns the vision-language correspondence through the video-to-text generation task. Meanwhile, the audio branch applies ImageBind as the backbone network and adds an Audio Q-former to project the audio signal into the embedding space of LLM. We train our VVLT model in a multi-stage manner. We first employ the large-scale video/image-caption corpus to perform the cross-modal alignment learning in a weakly supervised manner, and then further perform the instruction-tuning on the high-quality moderate-scale multi-modal datasets.

Our results show that Video-LLaMA is able to understand the content of complex video streams and generate relevant and context aware outputs that integrate visual and audio information. We open sourced the code and pre-trained model weights of our work to serve the purpose of providing a solid foundation for the construction of AI assistants that leverage audio-visual input.

Most recent works on multi-modal approaches to LLMs are limited to one additional modality, such as images or audio, unlike many natural and human constructed environments that show complex visual scences and are accompanied by audio. document.input:
We present Video-LLaMA, a multi-modal framework designed to empower Large Language Models (LLMs) with the ability to perceive and comprehend both visual and auditory content in video. Unlike previous research that typically focuses on a single additional modality—such as image or audio—Video-LLaMA bridges the gap by tackling two fundamental challenges: capturing temporal dynamics in visual scenes and integrating synchronized audio-visual signals.

To achieve this, we propose a dual-branch architecture. The vision branch utilizes a Video Q-former to adapt a pre-trained image encoder for temporal data, trained via a video-to-text generation task to establish vision-language correspondence. The audio branch leverages ImageBind as a foundation, incorporating an Audio Q-former to map auditory signals into the LLM’s embedding space. To ensure seamless integration, we employ a multi-stage training strategy: we first bootstrap cross-modal alignment using large-scale video/image-caption pairs, followed by instruction-tuning with high-quality, moderate-sized multi-modal datasets. 

Our results demonstrate that Video-LLaMA effectively perceives complex video sequences and generates grounded, meaningful responses based on both what it "sees" and "hears." By open-sourcing our code and model weights, we aim to provide a robust prototype for future audio-visual AI assistants.
